title: Contract QP vs In-House QP: Cost, Risk and Control
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: Contract QP vs in-house: compare cost, compliance risk and control for UK and EU batch certification, and choose the right Qualified Person model for your site.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette layered over deep navy, with a single subtle green accent glow. Soft natural light falls across a pristine quality-control laboratory bench where gloved hands rest beside a sealed batch-release document folder and a sleek tablet displaying abstract data charts; a row of softly blurred amber and clear vials sits out of focus in the background. Shallow depth of field draws the eye to the documentation and the precise, considered gesture of certification. Cinematic, premium, sterile yet warm, immaculate surfaces with gentle reflections, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: Is a contract QP legally equivalent to an in-house QP?
faqs.a: Yes. The legal accountability for batch certification under EU GMP Annex 16 and UK regulation is identical whether the Qualified Person is employed or engaged on contract. The QP must be named on the Manufacturer's/Importer's Authorisation in both cases, and the personal, non-delegable responsibility for the certification decision is the same. The MHRA applies no lower standard to a contracted QP.

faqs.q: Does using a contract QP mean we lose control over batch release?
faqs.a: No, provided governance is set up correctly. Control comes from documented authority, unfettered access to batch records and quality data, and a clear technical agreement defining scope and response times. In practice a well-drafted contract arrangement often gives sharper role definition than an informal in-house setup. The QP must always retain independent judgement on certification regardless of employment model.

faqs.q: When is a contract QP more cost-effective than hiring in-house?
faqs.a: A contract QP is usually more efficient for lower or variable batch volumes, importation-only operations, start-ups awaiting MIA approval, or sites bridging a vacancy or leave. You pay for defined certification activity rather than a fully loaded permanent salary plus cover. High-volume sites certifying batches daily generally justify an employed QP on per-batch economics.
